_comment: PHASE C-2 — ANTIETAM / SHARPSBURG, September 17 1862 — the FIRST MULTI-PHASE epic for the __FIELD real-time engine (DECISIONS D74/D76). Three sequential phases fought in one scenario via the gated T8 phases[] engine: PHASE 1 the CORNFIELD (dawn, the Confederate left — Hooker's I Corps + Mansfield's XII Corps vs Jackson's wing + Hood's counterattack, 'Artillery Hell'); PHASE 2 the SUNKEN ROAD / 'Bloody Lane' (mid-morning, the center — Sumner's II Corps vs D.H. Hill in a worn farm-lane trench, carried by Barlow's enfilade + a misheard Confederate order); PHASE 3 BURNSIDE'S BRIDGE (afternoon, the Confederate right — the IX Corps bottleneck across the Lower/Rohrbach Bridge, held for hours by Toombs's few Georgians, the breakthrough toward Sharpsburg halted by A.P. Hill's Light Division force-marching from Harpers Ferry). Each phase is its own sector/OOB/objective; the running casualty tally + the per-phase sector results carry forward; the overall battle is scored as a weighted aggregate with a draw band (the historical TACTICAL DRAW / marginal-Union-strategic-check emerges). COORDINATE NOTE: each phase field is 1200x900 yards; per engine convention the ATTACKER (US, every phase) sits at HIGH z facing -z (facing 0) and the DEFENDER (CS) at LOW z facing +z (facing PI); the objective sits at low-center z. ARTILLERY uses the D75 UNIVERSAL GUN MODEL (real gun counts + realistic crew; fire = guns * the universal weight). ANTI-LOST-CAUSE: the lessons are command failure on BOTH sides, terrain + contingency over martial 'genius', the appalling cost (the bloodiest single day), McClellan's wasted ~2:1 advantage + withheld reserves, and ABOVE ALL the Emancipation Proclamation five days later that turned the war against slavery. Strengths are Inferred division/brigade-scale approximations (Carman/Sears/OR differ); the Verified, teaching-bearing facts are the phase sequence, the commanders + their fate, the terrain, and the casualty weight. Sources: NPS Antietam, American Battlefield Trust, the Official Records (via aotw.org), Sears 'Landscape Turned Red', McPherson 'Crossroads of Freedom'.

sources: NPS Antietam National Battlefield (the Cornfield, S.D. Lee's 'Artillery Hell', the Bloody Lane, the Final Attack)
American Battlefield Trust (casualties 12,401 US / 10,316 CS / 22,717 total)

the Official Records via Antietam on the Web (S.D. Lee's 'twelve guns, all that could be manned'; Battery B 4th US — Campbell wounded, command to Lt. Stewart; A.P. Hill's report — in motion 7:30 a.m., reached the field ~2:30 p.m.)
Stephen W. Sears, "Landscape Turned Red: The Battle of Antietam" (1983)
Emerging Civil War (the revisionist A.P. Hill reading)
Ted Ballard, "Staff Ride Guide: Battle of Antietam" (U.S. Army Center of Military History)
American Battlefield Trust — Antietam Cornfield map narrative ("rain had stopped, leaving a thin ground fog"; hollows filled with mist)

weather.note: Dawn in the Cornfield opened in ground fog and mist that pooled in the hollows after overnight rain; it thinned as the sun rose over South Mountain.
sources: Stephen W. Sears, "Landscape Turned Red: The Battle of Antietam" (1983) — documents the overnight rain
American Battlefield Trust — Antietam Cornfield map narrative ("rain had stopped, leaving a thin ground fog"; hollows filled with mist)
Ted Ballard, "Staff Ride Guide: Battle of Antietam" (U.S. Army Center of Military History)

antietam.name: Antietam — the Bloodiest Day
antietam.place: Sharpsburg, Washington County, Maryland — along Antietam Creek
antietam.blurb: Lee's first invasion of the North is brought to battle along Antietam Creek. In one day — the bloodiest in American history, ~23,000 fell — three separate struggles rage across the rolling farmland: the dawn slaughter in Miller's Cornfield, the midday horror of the Sunken Road, and the afternoon fight for the stone bridge on the Confederate right. McClellan holds the Lost Order and nearly two-to-one numbers; Lee holds a thin line with his back to a single ford. Command the day, phase by phase — and learn why a near-run, badly managed bloodbath gave Lincoln the moment to free the slaves.
antietam.provenance: Verified (high) for the phase sequence, the commanders and their fate, the terrain, and the casualty weight; Inferred (medium) for per-unit strengths and exact arrival minutes (Carman, Sears, and the Official Records differ — all corps/division totals are contested estimates). Sources: NPS Antietam National Battlefield (the Cornfield, S.D. Lee's 'Artillery Hell', the Bloody Lane, the Final Attack); American Battlefield Trust (casualties 12,401 US / 10,316 CS / 22,717 total); the Official Records via Antietam on the Web (S.D. Lee's 'twelve guns, all that could be manned'; Battery B 4th US — Campbell wounded, command to Lt. Stewart; A.P. Hill's report — in motion 7:30 a.m., reached the field ~2:30 p.m.; the 16th CT mustered Aug 24 1862; the 9th NY's 240/373 loss; the 51st NY & 51st PA carrying the bridge); HistoryNet; Emerging Civil War (the revisionist A.P. Hill reading); Sears, 'Landscape Turned Red'; McPherson, 'Crossroads of Freedom'. ANTI-LOST-CAUSE: the lessons are command failure on BOTH sides, terrain + contingency over martial 'genius', the wasted Union advantage, and the Emancipation Proclamation — Confederate survival traces to McClellan's piecemeal method, an accidental ditch, a 12-foot bottleneck, an unscouted ford, and a misheard order, NOT innate Southern prowess.
antietam._weaponsNote: E46 WEAPONS PROVENANCE (all per-unit weapon tags Inferred, not Verified): the Army of Northern Virginia at Antietam was MAJORITY-RIFLED — ~70% rifled / ~30% smoothbore is the working estimate (HistoryNet estimate; AOTW 'Enfield very common'; the ANV ordnance chief's own arc has smoothbores not gone until Gettysburg) — but the basis is single-estimate-thin and the engine's binary tag forces per-unit modeling choices that no source resolves. Modeled: 75.8% of CS infantrymen rifled (the top of the band). The smoothbore minority is the lane garrison (Rodes + G.B. Anderson — D.H. Hill's division's .69 buck-and-ball close defense is the best-documented smoothbore case on the field), Toombs's Georgians (no sourced upgrade), and A.P. Hill's two units (the dossier bars singling them out for upgrades as weakly sourced — kept smooth AGAINST Confederate interest in a phase the CS already holds). US units stay high-rifled with Battery B's Napoleons correctly smoothbore.

menu.title: ANTIETAM (1862)
menu.deck: The bloodiest single day — a MULTI-PHASE epic. Fight the Cornfield, the Sunken Road, and Burnside's Bridge in sequence; survivors and the running cost carry forward. Command either army across all three sectors of September 17, 1862.
menu.aria: Antietam, September 1862 — the bloodiest day, a multi-phase real-time battle in three sectors: the Cornfield, the Sunken Road 'Bloody Lane', and Burnside's Bridge. Command either army through all three phases.

sides.heading: Antietam — Take Which Command?
sides.intro: September 17, 1862 · the bloodiest single day. Three sectors, fought in sequence. Lead the army of your choosing.

US.title: Command the Union (McClellan's army)
US.deck: Break the Confederate line in all three sectors — the Cornfield, the Sunken Road, the bridge. Do what McClellan would not: commit your weight and destroy Lee's outnumbered, river-pinned army.

CS.title: Command the Confederacy (Lee's army)
CS.deck: Hold a thin gray line all day across three sectors with your back to a single ford — the Cornfield, the lane, the bluffs above the bridge — and survive to fight another day.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. Three phases play in sequence; the cost carries forward (press V in battle to toggle fog).

brief.attack: Three sectors, one terrible day. Carry the Cornfield, storm the Sunken Road, and force the bridge before A.P. Hill's men reach the field. This is your war: McClellan had the Lost Order and nearly two-to-one numbers and let Lee escape — commit your weight and end the rebellion here.
brief.defend: Three sectors, one thin line. Hold the Cornfield, the lane, and the bluffs above the bridge long enough for the day to end and the army to escape across the Potomac. This is your war: survive the bloodiest day, as Lee's army did.

phases.name: The Cornfield
phases.sector: the Confederate left — Miller's Cornfield, the East & West Woods, the Dunker Church plateau

transition.title: The Cornfield
transition.lead: It is not yet seven in the morning. Hooker's guns open along the Hagerstown Pike; the corn stands shoulder-high between the lines. By nine o'clock it will be cut level, as with a scythe, and the dead will lie in rows where they stood.

hills.name: the Dunker Church plateau
hills.note: The low rise around the whitewashed Dunker (German Baptist) Church and the West Woods edge — the contested high ground and S.D. Lee's artillery position. (Verified)

hills.name: Nicodemus Hill
hills.note: West of the pike; Confederate horse-artillery (Stuart's guns) enfiladed the Union right from here. (Verified)

woods.note: MILLER'S CORNFIELD — the 24-acre field of standing corn (lethal concealment; the corn was cut level by the firing). Models as cover/concealment over the killing ground. (Verified ~24 acres, NPS/ABT — sources vary 24-30.)
the WEST WOODS — where Sumner's Sedgwick division would later be shattered in minutes; the Confederate left's cover. (Verified)
the EAST WOODS — the Union left-center approach; Mansfield mortally wounded near here. (Verified)
the NORTH WOODS — Hooker's I Corps forming ground at dawn. (Verified)

markers.name: the Hagerstown Pike
markers.note: The axis of the dawn assault, lined with post-and-rail fences that channeled the fighting and the canister. (Verified)

markers.name: the Dunker Church
markers.note: The pacifist German Baptist meetinghouse that became the Confederate left's anchor and the iconic landmark of the morning fight.

markers.name: Miller's Cornfield — 'every stalk cut as with a knife'
markers.note: The 24-acre cornfield that changed hands repeatedly; ~8,000 fell in and around it in some three hours.

leaders._note: fate = a per-leader durability multiplier weighting the casualty model to HISTORY (>1 endures, <1 is fall-prone). At the Cornfield, Mansfield was mortally wounded and Starke killed; Hooker was wounded in the foot; Lawton wounded; Hood's division wrecked. The others survived.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Joseph Hooker
US.note: I Corps; opened the assault down the pike. Wounded in the foot mid-morning and carried from the field, which cost the Union attack its driving hand. He survived. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Hooker is hit in the foot and carried off — the dawn assault loses its momentum at the worst moment.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Joseph K. F. Mansfield
US.note: XII Corps; one of the oldest generals on the field (58), in command only two days. Mortally wounded near the East Woods riding forward to steady his green regiments; died the next day. Williams succeeded him. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Mansfield is mortally struck near the East Woods — fifty-eight years old, two days in command, and gone.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Thomas J. 'Stonewall' Jackson
CS.note: Commanded the Confederate left after capturing Harpers Ferry. His thin line survived the morning by McClellan's piecemeal commitment as much as by its own discipline — NOT invincibility. He survived. (Verified; anti-Lost-Cause framing)
CS.teach: Gen. Jackson steadies the left near the Dunker Church — but the gray line holds as much by the enemy's piecemeal attacks as by its own valor.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. John B. Hood
CS.note: Committed his division from reserve near the Dunker Church ~7 a.m. — his men caught cooking their first hot rations in days. They counterattacked through the corn and were wrecked (~60% down; the 1st Texas lost ~82%). Asked where his division was, Hood answered: 'Dead on the field.' That is the cost, not a boast. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Hood's division counterattacks through the corn and is annihilated to buy minutes — 'Dead on the field,' he will say. The price of a charge, not its glory.

US.name: Union ammunition train — the North Woods

CS.name: Confederate train — behind the West Woods

US.name: Doubleday's Division (the Iron Brigade)
US.note: 1st Div, I Corps, incl. Gibbon's Iron Brigade (2nd/6th/7th Wisconsin, 19th Indiana). Drove down the pike's west side into the corn; the Iron Brigade's ~348 casualties anchor the strength estimate. (Verified identity; Inferred strength ~2,900 engaged — E46 accurate-inputs trim from 3,200, AOTW/Carman-derived I Corps figures)

US.name: Ricketts's Division
US.note: 2nd Div, I Corps; attacked through the East Woods and the corn and lost roughly a third of its strength in the morning. (Verified identity; Inferred strength ~3,100 engaged — E46 trim from 3,200, AOTW/Carman-derived I Corps figures)

US.name: Meade's Division (Pennsylvania Reserves)
US.note: 3rd Div, I Corps — the Pennsylvania Reserves, the THIRD of Hooker's three divisions in the dawn assault (center, astride the pike between Doubleday and Ricketts). The E46 audit found this division MISSING entirely from the model — the single largest erasure of McClellan's numerical weight in the phase. AOTW/Carman: ~3,131 infantry with 12 guns, ~2,855 engaged; modeled 3,100. Meade succeeded to I Corps command when Hooker was wounded. (Verified identity + presence; Inferred strength — E46 addition)

US.name: Battery B, 4th U.S. Artillery
US.commander: Capt. J. B. Campbell (w), then Lt. James Stewart
US.note: Fired double canister up the Hagerstown Pike near Miller's barn — some of the most famous gunnery of the war. Campbell was wounded early and Lt. Stewart directed much of the fire (credit both). Universal gun model (D75): 6 guns, ~100 crew. (Verified - six 12-pounder Napoleons, SMOOTHBORE; the double-canister role is the smoothbore signature - weapon tag corrected rifled->smooth.)

CS.name: Lawton's Division
CS.note: Ewell's old division, holding the Cornfield front at dawn; wrecked (about half down) before Hood relieved it. Lawton wounded and carried off. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: J. R. Jones's Division
CS.note: Jackson's own division, in the West Woods sector. Jones stunned and concussed early; Brig. Gen. William Starke (succeeding to command) killed — the Confederate defense was itself disjointed, not a serene wall. (Verified)

CS.name: S. D. Lee's Artillery Battalion
CS.note: Five batteries (Woolfolk, Parker, Rhett, Jordan, Moody) massed on the Dunker Church plateau — 'Artillery Hell.' Universal gun model (D75): 12 servable guns (Lee's report: 'twelve guns, all that could be manned'), ~300 crew; 86 casualties, 60 horses killed. Answered ~20 Union rifled guns firing crossfire from across Antietam Creek. (Verified)

CS.name: Stuart's Horse Artillery (Nicodemus Hill)
CS.note: ~15 guns massed on Nicodemus Hill under the 24-year-old Pelham (Stuart Horse Artillery + sections of the Staunton, Alleghany, and a Danville battery), enfilading Hooker's dawn assault from the west — the terrain already carried this Verified position; E46 put the guns themselves on the board. Displaced to Hauser's Ridge mid-morning under counter-battery fire. Universal gun model (D75): 15 guns (sources band 13-19; the ~40-gun figure is cumulative across the whole left-flank day), ~255 crew. Mixed rifles and smoothbores tagged by the enfilade role. (Verified position + command; Inferred count)

reinforcements.name: Hood's Division (the Texas Brigade)
reinforcements.entry: from reserve at the Dunker Church — the counterattack
reinforcements.note: Texas Brigade (Wofford) + Law's Brigade, ~2,300. Counterattacked through the corn ~7 a.m. and drove the Federals back to their start line, then was wrecked (~60% down; 1st Texas ~82%, colors lost). (Verified identity; Inferred strength — E46 raise from 2,000; the ~60% casualty figure is well-corroborated)

reinforcements.name: Williams's Division (XII Corps)
reinforcements.note: 1st Div, XII Corps; came up after the I Corps assault stalled. Williams succeeded to corps command when Mansfield was mortally wounded. (Verified identity; Disputed strength — E46 raise from 2,500 to ~4,600: the XII Corps engaged ~7,100-7,200 total and Williams's division was materially the LARGER of its two, vs Greene's ~2,500; corps-level figures range to ~8,000 across sources)

reinforcements.name: Greene's Division (XII Corps)
reinforcements.note: 2nd Div, XII Corps; pushed to the Dunker Church plateau, forced S.D. Lee's guns back, and held the high-water mark for about two hours before being driven out for want of support — the un-exploited breakthrough. (Verified identity; Inferred strength ~2,500 — the consistently-cited figure for Greene's small division; E46 kept it near the prior 2,400 rather than inflating it, with the corps' missing weight restored to Williams)

reinforcements.name: Early's Brigade
reinforcements.entry: up from the West Woods to restore the left
reinforcements.note: Took command of Lawton's division after Lawton fell; helped restore the Confederate left and retake the plateau as Greene's unsupported division was driven back. (Verified)

teaching.head: The most savage field of the war
teaching.body: Some 8,000 men fell in and around this cornfield in about three hours. It was command failure on both sides, not glory: McClellan fed in the I Corps, then the XII Corps, piecemeal — never together — so Jackson's thin line survived by his opponent's method. Hood's division was annihilated to buy minutes. The corn was cut level by the firing, and the dead lay in rows where they had stood.
teaching.provenance: Verified (high) — NPS; American Battlefield Trust; the Official Records.

phases.name: The Sunken Road (Bloody Lane)
phases._e46Note: HONEST DIVERGENCE (E46, D243; RE-ATTEMPTED D264 with E48+E49a+E49b live, then D276 with E53-v2+E54 pocket-collapse live — STILL OPEN): the Confederate batteries on the Piper plateau (Miller's Washington Artillery, Hardaway, Boyce, Carter — Verified presence, ~8-gun documented floor) are deliberately NOT modeled in this phase yet. The D276 re-attempt restored them as cs_piper_guns at the sourced farm seat (z 212, outside the objective ring) behind the unchanged both-seed-set A/B, and the lane's documented fall still missed the locked gate: P1 Sunken Road US 7/8 base -> 5/8 with Piper guns on both default and probe sets, below the required US >=6/8. WHY (measured, not inferred): E53-v2 improved the D264 red from 1-2/8 to 5/8, but the universal model still does not make the Barlow-wheel capture/collapse decisive enough with the documented gun line present; captured stayed 0 on every D276 Antietam row, and the no-shedding guard was 2/8 default and 5/8 probe. Honest ratio note (logged, NEVER a license — D74/SL-7): with the guns the battle-level attacker-pays ratio lands 1.07/1.05 vs base 0.93/0.92 and the historical ~1.20, so cost moves toward history while the direction gate remains short. Revert-file-halt executed; restore cs_piper_guns only behind the same seeded A/B gate if a later universal mechanism makes P1 US >=6/8 without tuning clocks, thresholds, weapons, morale, casualty math, or per-battle constants.
phases.sector: the Confederate center — the worn farm lane between the Roulette and Piper farms

transition.title: The Sunken Road
transition.lead: The fight shifts to the center. D.H. Hill's men crouch in a worn farm lane, sunk several feet below the fields, fence rails piled on its lip — an accidental trench. Sumner's II Corps must cross the open rise into its fire. By one o'clock the lane will be choked with the dead, and history will call it the Bloody Lane.

hills.name: the ridge before the lane
hills.note: The open rise the Union II Corps had to crest to reach the lane — every line was silhouetted against the sky as it came over. (Verified)

hills.name: the high ground on the flank
hills.note: Where Barlow wheeled the 61st & 64th New York to enfilade the length of the lane — the maneuver that broke it. (Verified)

woods.note: Roulette-farm orchard cover on the Union approach.
the Piper farm — the Confederate rear and artillery position, behind the lane.

walls.note: THE SUNKEN ROAD — a pre-war farm lane worn several feet below grade, with fence rails piled along its forward edge: an ACCIDENTAL trench giving the defender heavy cover. The lethality is TERRAIN, not martial superiority. This is the defender's hold line. (Verified)

markers.name: the Sunken Road (the Bloody Lane)
markers.note: The worn farm lane that became a ready-made trench — and then a mass grave when its defenders were enfiladed out of it. (Verified)

markers.name: the Roulette Farm (Union approach)
markers.note: The II Corps crossed the Roulette farm and orchard to reach the open rise before the lane.

markers.name: the Piper Farm (Confederate rear)
markers.note: Behind the lane; D.H. Hill's reserve and artillery, and the line of any Confederate retreat.

markers.name: Mumma Farm — burned that morning
markers.note: Confederates burned the Mumma farm at dawn to deny its buildings to Union sharpshooters.

leaders._note: fate: at the Sunken Road, Richardson was mortally wounded (the price of carrying the lane); Rodes and G.B. Anderson (CS) were wounded/mortally wounded; the others endured. Apply the 'real men, not romance' standard EVENLY — to Rodes's Alabamians and Anderson's North Carolinians as to the Irish Brigade.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Israel B. Richardson
US.note: 1st Div, II Corps; drove the attack that finally carried the lane. Mortally wounded by a shell fragment as he brought up a gun; taken to the Pry House, died Nov 3, 1862. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Richardson is struck down by a shell fragment at the moment of victory — the hardest-driving general on the field, gone.

US.name: Brig. Gen. William H. French
US.note: 3rd Div, II Corps; opened the assault on the lane ~9:30 a.m. and was repulsed with about a third of his division down. He survived. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. French's division is thrown back from the lane — a third of his men down on the open rise.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Daniel Harvey Hill
CS.note: Held the Confederate center in the lane with about 2,500 men. The position's strength was the worn ditch and piled rails — ordinary soldiers multiplied by terrain — not invincibility. He survived. (Verified; anti-Lost-Cause framing)
CS.teach: Gen. D.H. Hill holds the center in the sunken lane — a thin line made deadly by an accident of terrain, not by destiny.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Robert E. Rodes
CS.note: Held the lane's center with his Alabamians; wounded. A misheard 'about face'/realignment order pulled part of his line out — and that error, as much as Barlow's enfilade, opened the gap in Lee's center. Contingency, not fate. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Rodes is wounded, and a misheard order empties part of the lane — the gap in Lee's center opens by accident, not design.

US.name: Union train — behind the Roulette farm

CS.name: Confederate train — the Piper farm lane

US.name: French's Division (II Corps)
US.note: 3rd Div, II Corps; the first assault on the lane ~9:30 a.m., repulsed with ~1,750 lost (about a third — which back-solves to a ~5,250-5,700 division). Many were green regiments in their first fight. (Verified identity; Inferred strength — E46 raise from 4,400; sources contest the band 4,400-5,700 and ~5,700 is the consistently-cited figure)

US.name: Battery K, 1st U.S. Artillery
US.note: Supported the II Corps assault on the lane. Universal gun model (D75): 6 guns, ~100 crew. (Verified - rank corrected: CAPTAIN at the battle, promoted Oct 26, 1861; was under-ranked as Lt.)

CS.name: Rodes's Brigade (in the lane)
CS.note: Rodes's Alabamians, crouched in the sunken lane's western stretch (z 388, hard behind the rail-topped bank at z ~405, within the cover band). Veterans; the position — not the men — was the killing machine. E46 de-double-count: this unit previously carried 2,400 as 'D.H. Hill's Division' ALONGSIDE the separately-modeled G.B. Anderson brigade (itself one of Hill's brigades), double-counting the lane at 3,700; the lane garrison was Rodes (~1,150) + G.B. Anderson — NPS: 'over 2,500 of D.H. Hill's Division' in the lane, NOT the division's 5,449 present-for-duty. Unit id kept stable for save/registry continuity. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: G. B. Anderson's Brigade
CS.note: North Carolinians holding the eastern stretch of the lane (with Rodes, the ~2,450-2,500-man lane garrison of D.H. Hill's division). Anderson struck near the ankle; the wound led to amputation and his death Oct 16, 1862. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: R. H. Anderson's Division
reinforcements.entry: up through the Piper farm to thicken the center
reinforcements.note: Came up ~10:00-10:30 a.m. to reinforce Hill, but the division (six small brigades) was committed DISJOINTEDLY and wrecked by noon — it did not save the lane; Anderson wounded bringing it up. The xp:1 and the delayed mid-phase arrival (behind the already-fighting lane garrison) model the disjointed commitment. (Verified identity; Inferred strength ~3,400 — E46 raise from 2,100, AOTW/Sears)

reinforcements.name: Richardson's Division (the Irish Brigade)
reinforcements.entry: on French's left — Meagher's Irish Brigade
reinforcements.note: 1st Div, II Corps; the Irish Brigade (63rd/69th/88th NY + 29th Mass) charged the lane and lost ~540 (~60%); Caldwell's brigade then relieved them. (Verified — the Irish Brigade at Antietam was these four regiments only.)

reinforcements.name: Caldwell's Brigade (Barlow's enfilade)
reinforcements.entry: wheeling onto the flank high ground
reinforcements.note: Col. Francis Barlow wheeled the 61st & 64th New York onto high ground enfilading the length of the lane — the maneuver that, with a misheard Confederate order, broke the center and took ~300 prisoners. The lane fell to a flank move + a command error, not to a frontal triumph. (Verified)

teaching.head: An accident of terrain, a misheard order — not destiny
teaching.body: About 5,500 fell in and around this lane. The defenders were ordinary soldiers multiplied by terrain — a worn ditch and fence rails — not Southern invincibility. The position fell to a Union flank maneuver plus a Confederate command error. And the deepest lesson is the breakthrough McClellan would not exploit: believing himself outnumbered when in fact he heavily outnumbered Lee, he withheld a fresh reserve of more than 20,000 men that might have split the Confederate army in two.
teaching.provenance: Verified (high) — NPS Bloody Lane Trail; American Battlefield Trust; the Official Records. The reserve figure is 'more than 20,000' (Franklin's VI + Porter's V Corps); McClellan's belief he was outnumbered is paired with the reality that he was not.

phases.name: Burnside's Bridge
phases.sector: the Confederate right — the Lower (Rohrbach) Bridge over Antietam Creek and the road to Sharpsburg
phases.timing: ~10:00 a.m. — dark (bridge carried ~1:00 p.m.; A.P. Hill's counterattack ~3:30-4:00 p.m.)

objective.name: the bluffs above the bridge & the road to Sharpsburg

transition.title: Burnside's Bridge
transition.lead: On the Confederate right, the IX Corps must force a narrow stone bridge over Antietam Creek. Four hundred Georgians on the bluffs above hold it for three hours against thousands — a twelve-foot bottleneck, an unscouted ford, and Union hesitation, not Southern magic. Carry the bridge and reach for Sharpsburg and Lee's only line of retreat — before A.P. Hill's men arrive from Harpers Ferry.

hills.name: the west bluffs
hills.note: The high, wooded ground above the western bank that Toombs's Georgians held, firing down on every attempt to rush the bridge. (Verified)

hills.name: the Sharpsburg ridge
hills.note: The rising ground toward the town; the road to Boteler's Ford — Lee's single line of retreat — ran behind it. (Verified)

woods.note: John Otto's cornfield and the wooded bluffs where A.P. Hill's counterattack struck and the green 16th Connecticut routed. (Verified)
timber along the western bank below the bluffs.

walls.note: the wooded BLUFFS above the bridge — heavy cover for the defending Georgians, who fired down on the chokepoint. Seated so the defender doctrine's forward face (obj.z 295 + obj.r*0.55 ~ 367) lands the holding line ON the bluff in cover (the Fredericksburg pattern), forcing the Union to assault UP into the bluff and the canister. Models the bottleneck's defensive advantage. (Verified)

markers.name: Antietam Creek
markers.note: The creek that channelized the whole assault — crossable only at the bridge and at fords the Union failed to scout in time. (Verified)

markers.name: the Lower (Rohrbach) Bridge — 'Burnside's Bridge'
markers.note: A three-arched limestone span ~125 ft long and only ~12 ft WIDE (completed 1836) — the twelve-foot bottleneck that let a few hundred defenders stall an entire corps for three hours. (Verified)

markers.name: Snavely's Ford (downstream)
markers.note: The downstream ford Rodman's division finally found and crossed ~1 p.m. — the flanking move that, with the bridge, forced Toombs off the bluffs. Burnside's failure to scout the fords sooner was a major Union lapse. (Verified)

markers.name: to Sharpsburg & Boteler's Ford (Lee's retreat)
markers.note: The town and Lee's single line of retreat across the Potomac, which the 9th NY's advance briefly threatened.

markers.name: John Otto's cornfield
markers.note: Where A.P. Hill's late counterattack struck the exposed Union left and the three-week-old 16th Connecticut broke.

leaders._note: fate: at the bridge sector, Rodman (US) was mortally wounded crossing at Snavely's Ford; A.P. Hill's brigadier Branch was killed and Gregg wounded; Toombs survived. A.P. Hill's arrival is reframed: he collapsed an already over-extended, unsupported, low-on-ammunition Union advance — the trigger, not a lone miracle.

US.name: Maj. Gen. Ambrose E. Burnside
US.note: IX Corps; funneled his men across the narrow bridge for hours when the creek was fordable elsewhere, then advanced slowly and halted after crossing — the delay let the Confederate right be rescued. He survived. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Burnside feeds his corps over the narrow bridge — and the hours lost there will cost the army its chance to cut Lee's retreat.

US.name: Brig. Gen. Isaac P. Rodman
US.note: 3rd Div, IX Corps; found and crossed Snavely's Ford downstream to flank the bluffs. Mortally wounded (a ball through the left lung) when A.P. Hill struck the exposed left; died Sept 20, 1862. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Rodman is mortally struck as A.P. Hill's men hit the flank — the officer who turned the bluffs falls with them.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Robert Toombs
CS.note: Held the bluffs above the bridge with ~400-550 Georgians (2nd & 20th Georgia) for more than three hours — a bottleneck defense, not a miracle of valor: only a few thousand attackers could press the twelve-foot span at a time. He survived. (Verified; anti-Lost-Cause framing)
CS.teach: Gen. Toombs holds the bluffs with a few hundred Georgians — because a twelve-foot bridge let only a handful of the enemy come on at once.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. A. P. Hill
CS.note: Force-marched his Light Division ~17 miles from Harpers Ferry (in motion 7:30 a.m., on the field ~2:30 p.m.) and struck the exposed Union left ~3:30-4:00 p.m. He did not work a miracle — he collapsed an over-extended, unsupported, low-on-ammunition advance, exploiting McClellan's refusal to reinforce. He survived. (Verified; reframed per Emerging Civil War)
CS.teach: Gen. A.P. Hill's division arrives from Harpers Ferry and strikes the flank — the trigger that topples an already-faltering, unsupported Union advance.

US.name: Sturgis's Division (Ferrero's Brigade)
US.note: 2nd Div, IX Corps; after Crook's and Nagle's brigades were repulsed, the 51st NY (Potter) and 51st PA (Hartranft) of Ferrero's brigade finally carried the bridge ~1 p.m. (Verified)

US.name: Benjamin's Battery (IX Corps guns)
US.note: Heavy 20-pounder Parrott rifles (Battery E, 2nd U.S.) firing from the high east bank to cover the crossing. Universal gun model (D75): 4 heavy guns, ~100 crew; long reach, no close canister spike. (Verified - corrected: he was a 1st LIEUTENANT at Antietam, the captaincy came later; and the battery had FOUR 20-pdr Parrotts, not six.)

CS.name: Toombs's Brigade (the bridge defense)
CS.note: 2nd & 20th Georgia (+ a 50th Georgia detachment), ~450 — '~400 muskets, fewer than 250 by the end' — hard on the wooded bluffs above the bridge (z 360, within the bluff cover band at z ~365). Held the bottleneck for over three hours. E46: the unit previously carried 1,100 as a self-admitted headcount proxy for the 12-foot-span bottleneck; the real strength is restored, with the bottleneck's delay represented by the bluff terrain-cover band and the hold-to-win timer, not by phantom men. (Verified identity + strength)

CS.name: D. R. Jones's thin line
CS.note: Nearly all Lee had left to hold the Sharpsburg ridge on the right — a thin line stretched to cover the town and the retreat road, until A.P. Hill's Light Division came up. Holds the eastern stretch of the bluff line (z 358, in cover). E46: raised from 2,000 so the division totals ~3,100 with Toombs's bridge detachment at its real 450 (sources bracket Jones's division ~3,000-3,311) — a redistribution, not a net change. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

CS.name: The Sharpsburg Ridge Guns
CS.note: D.R. Jones's organic ridge artillery against the IX Corps push — the Washington Artillery companies (Squires's 1st, Richardson's 2nd, with Miller's 3rd rejoining from the Piper fight) falling back along the Harpers Ferry Road line, and Brown's Wise (VA) battery. E46 put the guns on the board (the phase previously modeled ZERO Confederate artillery); scoped to Jones's ~12 organic guns minus afternoon attrition — A.P. Hill's battalion (McIntosh, Pegram, Crenshaw) is deliberately EXCLUDED here because it is already narrated with Archer's reinforcement, and Garden's battery is excluded as unverified. Universal gun model (D75): 10 guns, ~170 crew. (Verified presence; Inferred count)

reinforcements.name: Rodman's Division (Snavely's Ford)
reinforcements.entry: across Snavely's Ford, onto the Confederate flank
reinforcements.note: 3rd Div, IX Corps; crossed downstream ~1 p.m. to flank the bluffs — incl. the 9th NY (Hawkins' Zouaves, who reached the edge of Sharpsburg and lost 240 of 373) and the three-week-old 16th Connecticut. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: Willcox's Division (toward Sharpsburg)
reinforcements.note: 1st Div, IX Corps; pushed up the slope toward Sharpsburg in the afternoon, nearly reaching Lee's line of retreat before the counterattack struck. (Verified)

reinforcements.name: A. P. Hill — Branch's & Gregg's Brigades
reinforcements.entry: from Harpers Ferry — onto the Union left flank
reinforcements.note: The leading brigades of Hill's Light Division after the ~17-mile march, striking the exposed Union left. Branch shot through the head and killed instantly; Gregg wounded; some men wore Union blue captured at Harpers Ferry, which made the green Connecticut regiments hesitate. E46: Hill's attack cut from a modeled 3,700 to his own report's total — 'the three brigades of my division actively engaged did not number over 2,000 men' — with this unit carrying the ~1,650 residual after Archer's ~350 (Pender/Field stayed in reserve, Thomas at Harpers Ferry). (Verified; arrival timing compressed to the phase clock — Hill must matter)

reinforcements.name: A. P. Hill — Archer's Brigade & Walker's guns
reinforcements.note: Archer's brigade — 'only 350 strong' after the straggling of the forced march — with Walker's artillery battalion (Pegram, Crenshaw, McIntosh): the guns opened first, then the infantry rolled up the exposed, unsupported Union left as the light failed. E46: cut from a modeled 1,500 to the sourced ~350 (the guns are narrated, not separately gun-modeled). (Verified identity + strength; the attached guns are Inferred texture)

teaching.head: Contingent, not destiny
teaching.body: A few hundred Georgians stalled an entire corps for three hours — but the cause was a twelve-foot bottleneck, an unscouted creek, and timid Union command, not innate Confederate prowess: only a few thousand attackers could press the span at a time. A.P. Hill's blow was the trigger that toppled an already over-extended, low-on-ammunition advance — exploiting McClellan's refusal to commit his reserves as much as rescuing Lee. Lee's army survived to recross the Potomac in the night.
teaching.provenance: Verified (high) — NPS Final Attack Trail; American Battlefield Trust; the Official Records (A.P. Hill's report); Emerging Civil War (the revisionist reading) alongside Sears.

US.US: History is overturned. You did what McClellan would not — you committed your weight across all three sectors and broke Lee's outnumbered, river-pinned army where the real Army of the Potomac let it survive and escape. In September 1862 this was a near-run, badly managed draw; under your command it became the decisive victory that might have ended the war.
US.CS: History is overturned. The Union broke your thin gray line across the sectors and pinned your army against the Potomac — the destruction Lee's army escaped in 1862, under your defense, it did not.

CS.US: History holds — the bloodiest day was a tactical draw, and Lee's army slipped away across the Potomac in the night. Like McClellan, you held the field but could not destroy a divided, outnumbered enemy. Yet turning back the invasion was a strategic Union check — and five days later Lincoln issued the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation.
CS.CS: History holds. Your thin line held across all three sectors through the bloodiest single day in American history, and your army lived to recross the Potomac in the night. But the invasion was turned back — a strategic Union check — and five days later Lincoln answered Sharpsburg with the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation that doomed Confederate hopes of foreign recognition.

endNote.draw: A tactical draw, as it was in history — the bloodiest single day in American history, and neither army driven from the field. Lee held on the 18th, then withdrew across the Potomac in the night. Turning back the invasion was a strategic Union check, and five days later it gave Lincoln the moment to issue the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation.

teaching.title: The Bloodiest Day — and the Turning Point

cards.head: The price, not the glory
cards.body: About 23,000 men were killed, wounded, or missing on September 17, 1862 — more Americans than fell in the War of 1812, the Mexican-American War, and the Spanish-American War combined, in a single day. Alexander Gardner's photographs of the dead in rows along the Sunken Road and the Hagerstown Pike were the first time Americans saw their own war's corpses. The Cornfield and the Bloody Lane are monuments to slaughter, not to maneuver.
sources: American Battlefield Trust (12,401 US / 10,316 CS / 22,717 total)
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — American Battlefield Trust (12,401 US / 10,316 CS / 22,717 total); NPS.

cards.head: Overwhelming advantage, wasted
cards.body: McClellan had the Lost Order — Lee's entire divided plan of march, found wrapped around three cigars — and nearly two-to-one numbers, with Lee pinned against the Potomac and a single ford. And still he attacked piecemeal, sector by sector, withheld more than 20,000 men in reserve, and let a divided, outnumbered army survive and escape. Advantages mean nothing without the will to use them. Lee survived as much by his opponent's caution as by his own skill — the opposite of 'Southern martial genius.'
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — Sears, 'Landscape Turned Red' (the piecemeal-commitment thesis); HistoryNet 'McClellan's Missed Opportunities'. Pair McClellan's belief he was outnumbered with the reality that he was not.

cards.head: Command failure on both sides, not heroism on one
cards.body: Confederate survival traces to terrain and contingency, not innate prowess: an accidental ditch in the center, a twelve-foot bottleneck on the right, an unscouted ford, captured blue uniforms, a misheard order that emptied the lane. And the Confederate defense was itself disjointed — Jones stunned, Starke killed, Lawton wrecked, Hood's division annihilated. There is no 'Jackson the wizard' here and no 'gallant outnumbered few' — only ordinary soldiers, terrible ground, and chance, on both sides.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — NPS; the Official Records; Emerging Civil War. The de-romanticizing standard applies evenly to Rodes's Alabamians and Anderson's Carolinians as to the Irish Brigade.

cards.head: Emancipation — the real significance
cards.body: Tactically September 17 was a brutal draw; Lee held the 18th, then withdrew. But turning back the first invasion of the North was strategic check enough for Lincoln, who had held the Emancipation Proclamation since July on Seward's advice not to look desperate. Five days later, on September 22, 1862, he issued the preliminary Proclamation. Antietam transformed the war from a struggle to restore the Union into a war against slavery, and made foreign recognition of a slaveholding Confederacy politically impossible. To dwell on Confederate tenacity while skipping this is to miss the entire point of why this day is remembered.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) — McPherson, 'Crossroads of Freedom'; American Battlefield Trust. Omitting emancipation from an Antietam treatment is the classic Lost-Cause erasure.

codex.title: Antietam / Sharpsburg, September 17, 1862
codex.summary: The bloodiest single day in American history — three sequential struggles (the Cornfield, the Sunken Road, Burnside's Bridge) along Antietam Creek. A tactical draw that turned back Lee's first invasion of the North; five days later it gave Lincoln the moment to issue the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation, redirecting the moral purpose of the war.
